caveats: a: BLS publishes this industry table at NAICS 237300 rather than the exact six-digit label, though its title and activities align closely with 237310.
a: Anthropic usage reflects one model ecosystem and observed digital interactions, not technically feasible automation or realized labor removal.
a: The estimate excludes already-automated work only judgmentally because no source isolates current automation by task in this industry.
rho: RICS respondents are global and only 10% came from the Americas.
rho: The survey covers construction broadly and is not restricted to LMM firms or highway work.
rho: Stated adoption does not establish durable labor substitution or avoidable hiring.
e: No source measures eligibility under this bespoke lens; this is a bounded business-model judgment.
e: The frozen firm count is an estimate, and NAICS assignment can reflect an establishment rather than a consolidated firm's full activity mix.
e: Repeat project revenue is less predictable than contracted recurring revenue.
s5: The FMI/CFMA sample spans engineering and construction, not highway contractors specifically.
s5: Survey respondents skew larger than the target: 44% reported revenue below $20 million and the remainder exceeded that threshold.
s5: Intentions are not completed transactions, and the source notes a full ownership and leadership transition can take eight to twelve years.
q: FHWA's 291-project study emphasizes alternative methods and completed projects and is not representative of all LMM contractor revenue.
q: Oregon law is one-state evidence, though it illustrates the low-bid mechanism common in public highway work.
q: The estimate does not double-count implementation failure or demand loss; it concerns only benefit sharing and repricing.
d5: Census spending is nominal, state-and-local, and year-to-date rather than a forecast or contractor-output index.
d5: FHWA scenarios are illustrative 20-year capital-need models and explicitly are not target forecasts.
d5: The five-year horizon crosses the end of IIJA authorization, making federal reauthorization a material uncertainty.
o: No source directly quantifies the five-year operator-required share.

o: Some maintenance tasks may move in-house or become more automated even while an accountable operator remains.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project and task-order revenue from public transportation agencies, municipalities, airports, general contractors, and private infrastructure owners, commonly awarded through competitive procurement and paid on unit-price, lump-sum, or mixed fixed-price terms; repeat work comes from successive projects, maintenance, rehabilitation, and on-call programs rather than subscriptions.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal construction units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and activities Census classifies elsewhere, including tunnel construction, highway lighting and signal installation, bridge painting, parking lots, and private driveways.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contractors providing recurring or repeat outsourced highway, street, road, airport-runway, public-sidewalk, and bridge construction, reconstruction, rehabilitation, repair, and directly related specialty work to public agencies, general contractors, and other external customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the 2023 employment and wage mix for NAICS 237300, while Anthropic measures task-level LLM exposure and usage across occupations, not wage-weighted substitution in highway contractors. I map substantial exposure to estimating, project controls, accounting, administration, document review, scheduling, compliance, and portions of management, apply smaller exposure to supervisory work, and little to physical trades and equipment work; this likely overstates substitution where field context and signed accountability remain necessary and understates multimodal progress-monitoring potential.
caveats: BLS publishes this industry table at NAICS 237300 rather than the exact six-digit label, though its title and activities align closely with 237310.
Anthropic usage reflects one model ecosystem and observed digital interactions, not technically feasible automation or realized labor removal.
The estimate excludes already-automated work only judgmentally because no source isolates current automation by task in this industry.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task diary from 20-30 highway contractors, split among estimating, project controls, finance, safety, field supervision, operators, and craft labor, paired with pre/post deployment time studies for bid takeoff, submittals, daily reports, change orders, and scheduling.
a.rationale: The occupation mix sharply limits language-model substitution: construction and extraction occupations are 64.65% of employment, including laborers at 23.35% and equipment operators at 19.02%. The exposed pool is concentrated in management (6.41%), business and financial operations (4.72%), office support (5.28%), and parts of engineering and supervision; cost estimators and project-management specialists alone are 1.46% and 1.63% of employment. A wage-weighted mapping therefore supports a materially exposed minority, not the majority of payroll.

d5.bridge: Census measures nominal state-and-local highway and street construction put in place through May 2026, while FHWA models long-run capital investment needs and backlog, not a five-year constant-price demand ratio for the current contractor service basket. I use near-flat recent spending as the starting point, deflate qualitatively, and treat the large repair backlog and economically justified spending range as downside support; post-IIJA authorization and state budget uncertainty widen both directions.
caveats: Census spending is nominal, state-and-local, and year-to-date rather than a forecast or contractor-output index.
FHWA scenarios are illustrative 20-year capital-need models and explicitly are not target forecasts.
The five-year horizon crosses the end of IIJA authorization, making federal reauthorization a material uncertainty.
d5.change_evidence: Enacted federal surface-transportation reauthorization for FY2027 onward, state DOT five-year letting plans, municipal capital budgets, Dodge/ConstructConnect project starts, and quarterly Census value-put-in-place deflated by FHWA highway construction cost indices.
d5.rationale: May 2026 year-to-date state and local highway-and-street construction was $48.359 billion versus $47.860 billion a year earlier, a 1.0% nominal increase, indicating roughly flat near-term real activity. Structural need is durable: FHWA estimates a $1.4 trillion 2022 investment backlog and $1.05 trillion repair backlog; constant real 2022 capital spending of $127.8 billion annually would reduce the total backlog 71.1% by 2042. The base is therefore near current real quantity with modest growth, not a boom forecast.

caveats: No source measures eligibility under this bespoke lens; this is a bounded business-model judgment.
The frozen firm count is an estimate, and NAICS assignment can reflect an establishment rather than a consolidated firm's full activity mix.
Repeat project revenue is less predictable than contracted recurring revenue.
e.change_evidence: A stratified review of 200 firms from a current 237310 business register, coding customer mix, repeat-project history, standalone workforce and equipment, operating status, captive status, and whether the enterprise could transfer independently.
e.rationale: Most firms in the code perform contracted construction, reconstruction, rehabilitation, repair, or related specialty work for external owners or primes and can win successive projects, so the default repeat-outsourced-service lens includes most LMM firms. The discount removes captive units, inactive or shell entities, firms dependent on a one-off project, joint-venture vehicles without transferable standalone operations, and misclassified or predominantly out-of-code businesses.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 237310 ($34,875,282,056) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 237310 ($156,121,994,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $508,629 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 237310); class <5: 3,194 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,103,210 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $87,815 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,409 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,258,996 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $259,416 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,217 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,333,961 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $504,183 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,954 firms, avg revenue/firm $19,677,270 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,566,311 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 531 firms, avg revenue/firm $89,893,964 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,155,560 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 136 firms, avg revenue/firm $397,903,721 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $31,673,136 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 2,485

caveats: No source directly quantifies the five-year operator-required share.
The estimate assumes the current physical service basket and does not treat productivity as demand elimination.
Some maintenance tasks may move in-house or become more automated even while an accountable operator remains.
o.change_evidence: State DOT and municipal procurement data tracking self-performed versus outsourced work, autonomous-equipment deployments with crew reductions, prime-contractor accountability clauses, and five-year changes in contractor labor and equipment intensity per constant-quality lane-mile or bridge project.
o.rationale: The service basket is physical construction, rehabilitation, and repair in hazardous, regulated, multi-employer work zones. Software can improve bidding, scheduling, monitoring, documentation, and machine control but cannot eliminate the accountable contractor that mobilizes labor and equipment, controls the site, delivers the asset, and carries safety and contract obligations within five years. A small downside allowance covers owner self-performance, modular methods, autonomous equipment, and narrower software-enabled service elimination.

q.bridge: FHWA and Oregon DOT describe highway procurement and contract forms, not the discounted share of an AI benefit retained by contractors. I infer retention from firm-fixed/unit pricing during a contract, then reduce it for transparent competitive rebidding, low-bid awards, owner negotiation, and eventual estimate normalization; fixed-price duration raises near-term retention, while repeated price competition lowers long-term retention.
caveats: FHWA's 291-project study emphasizes alternative methods and completed projects and is not representative of all LMM contractor revenue.
Oregon law is one-state evidence, though it illustrates the low-bid mechanism common in public highway work.
The estimate does not double-count implementation failure or demand loss; it concerns only benefit sharing and repricing.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level gross-margin and bid-spread data from contractors before and after AI deployment, segmented by unit-price, lump-sum, CM/GC, design-build best-value, task-order, contract duration, renewal, and number of qualified bidders.
q.rationale: Contractors can retain workflow savings during an awarded fixed-price or unit-price job, especially when fewer estimating and administrative hours do not alter measured quantities. Retention erodes on subsequent bids because conventional design-bid-build often rewards lowest price, estimates and unit prices are legible, and agencies can negotiate or benchmark alternatives. FHWA's completed-project sample was 47% design-bid-build, 14% design-build/low-bid, 27% design-build/best-value, and 12% CM/GC, supporting a mixed but price-disciplined outcome.

rho.bridge: RICS surveyed more than 2,200 global construction professionals about organization-level AI adoption and barriers, not US LMM highway contractors or the share of exposed labor implemented within five years. I translate today's low deployment, the 34% in pilots, and the stated integration, skills, data, cost, and ROI barriers into a five-year implementation fraction; the global and professional-heavy sample may overstate small-contractor readiness, while rapid tool embedding may make it understate five-year diffusion.
caveats: RICS respondents are global and only 10% came from the Americas.
The survey covers construction broadly and is not restricted to LMM firms or highway work.
Stated adoption does not establish durable labor substitution or avoidable hiring.
rho.change_evidence: A US-focused annual survey of 237310 firms by revenue band that records production deployment, workflow-level labor hours saved, error/rework rates, system integration, approved budgets, and abandoned pilots, plus audited case studies over at least 12 months.
rho.rationale: Current adoption is immature: 45% reported no implementation, 34% early pilots, just under 12% regular use in specific processes, 1.5% use across multiple processes, and less than 1% organization-wide embedding. Yet targeted use cases match exposed contractor workflows. Five years should permit meaningful implementation, but fragmented systems, weak data, human verification, public-contract documentation, and change management keep the base below one-half of exposed opportunity.

s5.bridge: FMI/CFMA measures stated exit intentions and ownership planning among almost 300 US engineering-and-construction executives, not realized qualifying control transfers among eligible 237310 LMM firms. I start below the reported 38% planning to exit in three to five years and discount for unrealized plans, internal continuity that may not change control, liquidation, and low third-party saleability; selection toward larger and more planning-engaged respondents could bias the source upward, while unplanned transfers bias it downward.
caveats: The FMI/CFMA sample spans engineering and construction, not highway contractors specifically.
Survey respondents skew larger than the target: 44% reported revenue below $20 million and the remainder exceeded that threshold.
Intentions are not completed transactions, and the source notes a full ownership and leadership transition can take eight to twelve years.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, BizBuySell, SBA loan, ESOP, and state contractor-license records matched to a denominator of eligible 237310 firms for 2021-2026, separating third-party control sales, family/management control transfers, ESOPs, liquidations, and non-control recapitalizations.
s5.rationale: The source reports that 38% of owners plan to exit in three to five years, but half of that group lacks an ownership-transition plan; 64% of respondents intend an internal transfer, and the report says only a small percentage of E&C firms are saleable to a third party. These facts support substantial succession-driven availability but a qualifying five-year control-transfer probability well below stated exit intent.

principal_driver: A durable physical repair-and-rehabilitation service basket supports demand while AI targets a smaller, higher-wage layer of estimating, project controls, and administration.
principal_weakness: Predominantly competitive public procurement makes productivity gains visible and likely to be shared through lower bids and renewal repricing.
